transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Audio.
Hey, there we go.
Did it.
Hello.
Hello.
How are you?
Good.
How's it going?
Good.
Good.
This is Char.
Hi.
Hi, Char.
Our copywriter.
How are the team?
Nice to meet you.
Nice to meet you as well.
I used to be a copywriter, so that was nice to meet fellow writers in the room.
There's a very specific version of writing that I just do not understand.
I don't know how you do yours.

Yeah, so I think maybe I think we have it later in the schedule in this meeting to walk through our brand documents, which should hopefully, I think, if anything, you know, you can tell us as you hear that what would make sense to sort of incorporate it into the pipeline.
fine.
Yeah,
And, you know, what we would need to kind of provide to make sure that that is sort of incorporated in the best way.
Yeah.
I think we had, should we get to that now?
Or was there a few other things on the agenda before we?
This was the biggest one for me on the agenda.
I'm looking forward to jumping into all of that.
I think the only other thing was we still need new topics approved, I think.
Okay.
And I know there's a few that are, I think all of the clusters have at least kind of five or six choices.
I know that there are a few that are light, so we're adding more to those.
Okay, great.
But my hope is that there's still enough, you know, runway there to set us up for at least for next week.
Yeah.
Do we have like a snapshot of which, I'm sure we do, which clusters we actually have approved topics for or articles for?
And if there's ones that are particularly light that I can, you know.
Yeah.
Pull up your air table.
And I can't see it now.
Okay.
So the interface that I set up sort of shows all the proposed topics, and it's broken out by cluster.
So you can see, I think because of the layers of research we did or keyword research, you know, focusing on AI for small business, some of those more recent ones are built out more.
And if you were lower on like home services, business, we need to build out more of those.
So, and then the small business.
Does this also capture which ones we already have in the works?
Just if there's, I feel like there's some clusters where we are very kind of over-indexed on blogs that we've created for.
Yeah.
Oh, like in terms of what's in production and much published.
Yeah.
So we have that data.
So why don't I just right after this, can spin up a new interface that just shows it to you like this.
Because the data is there, but I feel like it'll probably be easier if I just pull it into a view where you can just kind of see it at a glance.
So I'll pull anything that's in review, currently in production, or already published.
Does that make sense?
And then you can get a sense of where we are across the clusters.
Perfect.
Yeah.
Okay.
Yeah, that should just help me guide a little bit what I'm doing.
Yeah, for sure.
Okay, great.
I think those were sort of have like the usual reporting snapshot, but nothing major to call out there.

And we did see a big jump in scrunch in competitive presence from 39 to 45, which is, you know, good news and exciting to see that you guys moved into the number one spot in the prompts that we're tracking.
Um, so, you know, all things we like to see, I think there was nothing, um, nothing.
Nothing urgent to call out.
I did have a question about the profit first content stream that you all have.
I know this is part of a partnership, but I was just wondering, it seems like it's kind of a steady performer.
was curious if that's kind of content that you can continue to publish if it's something either that content or adjacent content would be something that we should look into creating more content on.
Yeah, probably.
Like you said, it continues to be a strong driver for us.
We still do have a pretty close partnership with profit first and profit first professionals and the creator of that.
And it still continues to be a source of new customers for us.
So it probably does make sense to explore either kind of replacing or incorporating this into one of the existing, one of the existing, yeah, pillars or category.
Okay.
Yeah.
I know we're sort of taking a look at the strategy, right, to evaluate, like, what's performing and what we should lean into.
So I was wondering, you know, kind of, yeah, what your approach was to this and whether that's something we should consider, too.
I think it would be a good idea.
I think we should because it works and we know people, yeah, it works for our audience.
And then in terms, like, so then what is the next steps in terms of kind of re-approaching the categories that we're looking at?

We kind of talked about it a little bit yesterday, but, like, one of the, I want to make sure that we're kind of expanding our horizons a little bit and not continue to kind of dip into the same buckets because it's something that's starting to feel a little samey.

And what the kind of best approach is to start thinking about how we can kind of broaden the type of topics that we're going after if there's, like, should I make some, like, because another thing that I'm noticing, like, if we look back at our top performing blogs, like older stuff.
Yeah.
A couple of them that just continue to perform really well are sort of outliers in terms.
So they're not really directly related to kind of small business banking or anything like that that we're kind of leaning into.
Like one of them was like, what is the G-Wagon tax write-off?
It was just a very kind of odd, you know, odd performer.
And then one is like how to make like $10,000 a month or something like that.
Like stuff like that that's a little more sort of tangential to the focus that we have as a business, but still kind of speak to a small business audience.
And I'm not sure the best way to kind of capture that as a cluster.
Yeah, I mean, I would be curious to see if there were kind of trends across those.

And also if you felt like they were outliers in terms of traffic, but like sometimes if you get a post that like has a very, not like clickbaity, but like it might bring in traffic that's not necessarily like the traffic that's going to convert for you.
So I would be curious if they feel like an outlier in terms of.
What your kind of core messages are and who your core audience is if they feel like kind of what your spidey sense says or what your conversion traffic says.
If it's like spiking high in traffic, but maybe not in intent that you sort of wouldn't want to build, I think, like, you know, strategic changes around that.

But if it feels like it's, yeah, like potentially capturing like a new, your audience and like a new avenue, I think, of traffic, then I feel like, yeah, like we would definitely want to take that into consideration, whether there are sort of things that we hadn't, places we could expand into that we hadn't really been considering.
Yeah, let me spend a little more time with those ones and just see if they're sort of a...

Yeah, like I would trust, you know, you know, you obviously know your audience and you know, you know, so, so I would be curious to get like your insights there on whether you thought that there was something valuable there we should be taking into account.
Yeah.
Yeah, and I'll take a look at the sort of the downstream.

Yeah, in terms of like how to approach, like, I think taking a look at what we've done so far, I mean, I know it's been, publishing has been a little bit slow because of the whole, you know, approval process.
So I think at this point, we're still sort of looking at positive signals, you know, and making projections towards the future.

So I think especially some of the ones where we haven't, like, published quite as much in, like, I'm always loathe to, like, look at the percentage change because the absolute numbers can be so small.
It doesn't always kind of tell the right story.
But I still think, you know, signals are signals.
So we can certainly, and I think what we've been doing, too, is we keep adding things.
Like, we added AI for small businesses.
We sort of added this accounting focus for a while.
So I think we can, like, take a look at of what we're doing.
Like, what are the areas that we feel like are gaining traction?
And when asked the question, you know, are those the places we want to double down?
Are there places that seem to not be getting traction?
Are those places where we want to, like, try something else?
Like, we had talked about some of those more, you know, the home services content, if that's, like, an area we want to experiment.

So I think it's on us to sort of, like, sort of what we talked about at the meeting yesterday, like, take a look at, like, basically give you a progress report of, like, how has it performed so far against the things you care about?
And then I think using that to make recommendations for, like, how we might want to adjust the strategy going forward feels to me like the kind of natural cadence.
So getting you next week that snapshot for January reporting and that progress report, because I think that'll be the baseline for the conversation of, you know, we can make recommendations.
I based on that, but I think it also depends on you and like what audience you really want to focus on.
And I know organic traffic is going to be like a big key metric this year as well.

But I think if there are other things you're focused on, like, you know, conversion within like a specific audience, or, you know, I think there are other things that might inform how we want to adjust the strategy.
Okay.
Does that make sense?
I feel like I just talked to you a lot.
No, no, no.
It does.
Yeah.
That, yeah.
It all feels good.
I know there's a lot happening right now and a lot kind of potentially changed direction, but I think we're at right now.
That feels good.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So I think on us, I think next week we'll get you the roll up for January and we'll also get you and Bethany that sort of overall progress report.
And I think we have a better sense after the conversation yesterday, what are the areas we really want to focus on for that progress report?
You know, what do you.
We want more visibility into.
So I think we'll get you those.
And then I think we, if there are any initial strategy recommendations that come out of that, we'll, of course, share those.
But then I think also kind of using that as a way to talk about how we might want to shift strategy going forward.
Cool.
Yeah, sounds good.
Yeah, so that was sort of it on mine.
So if you guys want to walk us through that or walk me, I'll share it with our editorial team, too.
But, yeah, through whatever documentation you have, that would be great.
Yes.
Take it away.
Just making sure I can.
Okay.
I feel like I haven't seen an updated version of this myself.
this is going to be...
New for everyone.
Yeah.
All right.
So this is our full brand book.
Obviously, only part of this will be relevant for you all.
And we can kind of parse out what's going to be relevant and send it, I'm assuming, is...
Yeah, I mean, I guess maybe you can kind of...
I recommend like which, how, like what would be the best sort of format from what you see here that is relevant to kind of get it into the proper systems.
But this is kind of new to me as well, so I'm not sure what that may be.
Cool.
Okay, so I will top line some of this more strategic stuff at the front.
So what is, we're calling our brand platform, which is kind of like our belief system, how we show up, who we show up for, how we define ourselves and our audience.
So first of all, I'm sure you've seen our mission, vision, position.
This just kind of sets the stage for why Relay exists and what we're ultimately trying to do, which is to make every business great with money.
And so it all kind of comes, everything that we're doing all kind of comes back to that idea.
We have a more fulsome, I'll say creative definition of who our audience is.
Because this is an R1 of this brand book, we're going to be building out actual.
Personas this year that are more specific in terms of like demographic information and things like that.
So this is kind of just like a vibe set of who our audience is and how we kind of want to speak to them and speak about them, which in this case is established small business owners.

Obviously, we have kind of the 1 million plus ICP, but this just kind of goes more into like their mindset and their mentality in terms of like craft and grit and tenacity and how we're kind of trying to meet them where they are there and provide solutions to build stronger businesses in the long run.
We also have a little bit of a top line into kind of the traits of our audience that we really value and celebrate and try and reflect in our brands.
So things like freedom and autonomy, grit and determination and community and camaraderie.
These are pillars that are important to our audience that we're going to try and speak to and show up in.
In.

Kind of our more creative campaign messaging, but can absolutely take inspiration from this in terms of, like, thinking about how you're framing and positioning blog articles and kind of the way you show up in those more educational moments.
Maybe there's a way to add some of that, like, grit and, like, just relevance around the realities of their day-to-day lives.

And then this is our, so I'm going to blast through this really quickly, so just pause and pause me and ask questions if you need to, but this is our kind of brand persona, so top-level intro into our personality, which is the Pathfinder.

So, essentially, we're saying that it's our job to clear the way forward for these small business owners to kind of, like, shine the flashlight ahead of them, show them what obstacles they might face, and clear them out of the way so that they can be great with money and build longer-lasting, more resilient businesses.
And we're kind of framing ourselves as this, like...

Competitive advantage against other people in the market, even big businesses in the market, because we're giving them the same insights, tools, and supports that they've always had so that these small businesses can kind of take up more piece of the pie, so to speak.
And then we get into the verbal identity, which is probably what's going to be the most helpful for your team.
This is kind of the more nitty gritty guidelines in terms of how we want to write and sound and feel in our writing.
So we've got personality, unwavering, upstanding, audacious, authentic.
We go into what that means more specifically in this next slide here.
So when we say unwavering, meaning confident, steady, optimistic, but not like rah-rah cheerleadery.
Upstanding is honest, expert, clear, but not like sugar-coated, condescending.
These are really capable people, and we're meeting them where they are and supporting them.
We are...
So audacious, bold, gritty, but not edgy for edgy's sake.
We're not going to go out and start cursing in our copy or anything like that.
But we're going to be upfront about, you know, running a business is really hard.
And we're not going to try and pretend that it isn't.
And we're going to say what other folks are kind of scared to say.
And then authentic, and I think this is the biggest one, is we're a bank, yes.
We're a tech company, yes.
But when we show up in our writing, we want to sound genuinely human and conversational and not, like, stiff and boring.
So how can we infuse some personality in what might be the more boring topics?
We define some tone here as well.
So obviously, voice is always going to stay consistent.
But how we show up in different channels might flex our tone.
This section here, probably most relevant for you all.
In terms of when we teach.
So when we're educating, our tone is steady, approachable, and confident.
So again, not using a whole bunch of jargon and complicated wording.
If there's a simpler way to say it, we're going to say it that way.
And we're going to just speak like a human about these topics and know that our audience knows what they're talking about.
And we're just kind of helping them and supporting them on the journey.
Can I pause you here and ask a question?
Of course.
I'm curious, and this may be more of a question for Dave.
I know we just sort of did a refresh of voice and tone because there was a feeling like that had kind of gone off the rails a little bit.
How do you feel like the recent posts have stacked up against your kind of this messaging around your voice and tone?
Are there particular places where we're falling short?
I find often my question is because I think our pipeline does best with incremental updates so we can test and see like.
What improvements it's made when we make a bunch of changes at once sometimes, then you don't exactly know like what's improving or what's not.
So I'm curious like what you would call out as the areas that maybe we still need to work on.

I think I need to spend a little more time with the most recent stuff because I'm kind of, you know, there was a point sort of with the sort of backlog of content that came in over the holidays where I felt like it really went in the wrong direction with feeling very kind of stiff and corporate and jargony.
And I think we've started to correct for that.

I know there's always going to be a balance between like how much of this kind of thing we can put into our blogs while still, you know, having them be optimized for what we want them to be optimized for.
So I think there's going to be a balance there.

But I think, you know, I guess I'd turn the question back on you to the aspect of how much is it realistic to be able to get to a point like this and still, you know, and still have it be optimized for.
SEO, and AEO, and, you know, all the things that we needed to be.
I mean, I think that some of what performs, like, is much more about how you're delivering the information.
So, like, you know, not sacrificing clarity for a tone, but I think that's something you wouldn't want anyway, regardless of kind of what the content was.

So I think for me, the parts about performance, I think, are much more about communication clearly, and then, you know, making sure the information's in there, and then, like, how the content is structured.

Like, making sure that, you know, I think sometimes if something's templated in terms of, like, how you teach something where you have sections that are, like, common mistakes and, like, what is ThingX, it's because, like, those clear chunks of content and, like, a definitional or educational article, you know, structuring that way is the way we found that works and, like, performs best for SEO.
And also, I think for AEO, chunking is really important, but I actually think that, you know, voice and...
Tone, there's a huge opportunity there to sort of make it feel more human and to, like, come at it from that empathy that you have for your audience.
So I think that, to me, feels like a place where we could absolutely, you know, continue to make improvements, I think, without, I don't think we'd sacrifice anything on the SEO and AEO front.
Yeah, that'd be great.
I mean, would, yeah, I think we have a, we're going for a very particular, you know, tone and voice.
And I think, I don't, I don't think we're currently capturing that, really, with, with, with the content that we're doing.

And I, for a lot of reasons, and I, you know, but I think there, if we can kind of incrementally get to a point where we're feeling a little more and more like that's being captured, I think that would, that would be fantastic.
And then, this is probably a question for both of you.
Do you have, and it can be stuff, obviously, that you've worked on internally.
Do you have blog posts that you feel like represent the gold star for what you're?
Going for in terms of voice and tone right now.
I know some of this work is recent, but.
Yeah, well, this work is quite recent.
So I think it's worth me kind of going back and seeing what we have of our recent kind of.
Bethany quoted the Cashflow Compass as a good example of being like the closest, but this is brand new work, like even today.
So where this has shown up mostly right now is in work that I've been doing on the marketing side.
So the new homepage will be a good example when it launches.
You probably have some stuff written that we could share.
And the Cashflow Compass is one that was, that you guys do have, that was kind of flagged early on as an example.
But yeah, anything else that.
But yeah, it's the incremental changes is fine.
Like this is new, new, new.
So as long as we can kind of make purposeful tweaks into like what you were saying, the way that you're kind of framing your content without sacrificing the clarity and the performance that you need.
I think that's a win.
Really, ultimately, what we're trying to do is just show up a little more consistently across everywhere versus, like, blogs sound one way, and then PR sounds another way.
Yeah, 100%.
So that's really what we're trying to do.

The reason I ask is because I found examples are really helpful, but for training AI systems, examples like this, which is kind of where we started, where it's, like, a good sentence and a bad sentence often aren't helpful or they're not as helpful because it loses the context between ideas.
So we found that being, like, here is a good paragraph or here is a good and a bad article are actually much more helpful for training the system.

So we'll definitely take this and we'll sort of make those updates and kind of recalibrate, but I think if you do have a piece or two of content that's, like, feels like a gold star, then that would be really, I think, helpful in helping us kind of accelerate and get there faster.
So if you have it, when you have it.
Send it our way.
Yeah, that's totally fair.
Totally fair.
And we do have pretty specific guidelines also in here, which are maybe a little more precise than would typically be in a brand book.
But because this is so new and because we try to enable as much self-service across our teams as we can, here they are.
So hopefully this helps as well.
And there are a decent amount of examples as well.
We'll send this to you so you can go through it.
But it's a lot of just like how we want to show up in our writing, like I was saying, keep a conversational, watch the jargon, things like that.
That shouldn't be too, too surprising, but we'll give you some good guard routes to bump up against.
This is probably the biggest one is, and I think the Cashflow Compass does this fairly well in terms of humor.
We're trying to figure out how we can show up and use humor without coming.

It's like too youthful or untrustworthy and kind of the way that we're defining it as this is kind of like a dry humor like what I was saying is like just confronting the realities of business ownership and saying it boldly and kind of to like catch people off guard kind of thing and disarm them and just embrace the realities of owning a business.
So this could be something, it might be tough to float into a blog article, but it's something you could try and play with in terms of how.
Yeah, I think like intro paragraphs, think in particular, aren't just like an easy place to kind of insert this kind of thing.
Yeah, yeah, for sure.
Yeah.
The rest of this might not, I mean, emojis, I don't know that you're using too many emojis.
I know you do use AI.
I will leave this with you and Dave for how you approach that.
This is just, oh.
I don't know what that is, but that's okay.

This is our internal guidelines in terms of how we're going to use AI, which is basically not to use it to write final publish-ready copy, but I'll leave it with you guys about what you're comfortable doing.
And then we do have a little writing review checklist here, which, again, just kind of summarizes everything we've gone through in terms of, like, would a real business owner say it this way?
Can I say this out loud without cringing, basically?
So, again, just another gut check around our tone and how we're hoping to show up.
The rest of this is going to be visual guidelines, which won't be relevant, but that's the quick and dirty intro into where we're going with voice and tone.
No, I think this all makes sense.
I think it's really clear, and we can definitely do a round of updates to our internal voice and tone guide.
I also agree that targeting that intro paragraph...
And, like, trying to hone that so it feels, I think we've talked about this, so it feels fresh and, like, we're, like, not repeating the same structures.
I'll be curious to see if we ask it to be wittier what we get.

That was, like, a little bit hard, I think, to, like, to, but I think sometimes it's, you know, it's valuable to just experiment a little, like, see if we take some of those guardrails off when it comes to that.
You know, you can always pull back a little bit.
So, yeah, I think I'll take this to our editorial team.
If you don't mind sharing it, if that's, I know you said it's internal, but if you're okay sending that to us.
We'll just pull out, we'll take the visual guidelines out.
The copy stuff is pretty locked.
One thing that I will say, and Dave and I haven't talked about this, but a change...
Change to our brand standard is sentence case over title case.
For headlines.
For everything.
Other than instances of five words or less where they're using the all caps, which is more of like a marketing side of things.
But yes, we are moving to sentence case for everything.
You can...
You have to go back and change a lot of blocks.
It's okay.
You can think about it for a minute, about how you feel about that in terms of a blog and title use case.
But everywhere else, we will be moving to sentence case.
So just think that over and then we can kind of decide how we want to tackle it in your channel.
But yeah.
Okay.
Yeah.
So for now, should we kind of stay on?
Well, I mean, obviously, we'll update all the voice and tone stuff.
Would you like us to start producing new content in sentence case or should we leave it as is for now?
Well, I mean, it might be a little premature.
I'm sure we'll probably end up going in that direction.
And if that's what...
That's what we're going to do elsewhere.
But again, we can do the blog is kind of its own thing.
Right.
So if it's different, it's different, you know?
Yeah.
I mean, I, I don't really care one way or another as long as we're consistent, but that's, that's all I, um, yeah.
That's your area of expertise.
Let's keep it, because now that we're going to end up, it's too much of a project right now to have to go back and like change everything because it's already in the works.
So let's keep it as is for now.
All So this is great.
So yeah, I'll walk our editorial team through, but I think it all looks pretty clear.
I think there's a lot there.
Um, so maybe what I'll do too, is ask them to update our voice and tone guide once you send that.
And I can just share that with you, that artifact directly.
Um, and you can see if there's anything that feels like important context to add in there.
Um, anything that we missed.
And then we'll start kind of using that in our next round of content, I guess it'd be next week.
Cause I think this week's.
Articles are done, but we can see what you think in terms of, you know, with a special eye to voice and tone.
Great.
Good.
All right.
Well, that was it for me.
It was nice to meet you, Char.
Likewise.
Yeah.
Thanks for letting me crash.
Yeah, of course.
And if you want to drop that, you can drop that, the guide in our Slack channel.
And if that's the easiest way or email it, it's too big up to you.
You can always give it to me if you're not in there, if you don't want have another Slack channel on you.
Oh, yeah.
I going to say, I'll just send it to Dave in the day of 10.
Yes, works.
Cool.
All right.
right.
Thank you so much.
Thanks, Thanks, Julie.
Bye.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Align on content strategy and integrate Relay's new brand guidelines.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=106.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Strategy Shift:** The strategy will shift to explore high-performing "outlier" topics (e.g., G-Wagon tax write-off) and the "Profit First" content stream, which drives new customers.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=302.0)
  - [**Brand Voice Integration:** New content will adopt the "Pathfinder" voice (unwavering, upstanding, audacious, authentic) to improve consistency and humanize the brand.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=976.0)
  - [**Performance Baseline:** GrowthX will deliver a January performance report and a full progress report next week to provide a baseline for the strategic review.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=621.0)
  - [**Headline Style:** Blog headlines will remain in title case for now, deferring a switch to sentence case to avoid a large, retroactive project.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=1674.0)

## Topics

### Content Strategy & Performance Review

  - **Performance:** Competitive presence in Scrunch increased from 39 to 45, securing the \#1 spot for tracked prompts.
  - [**Strategic Opportunities:**](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=302.0)
      - [**"Profit First" Content:** This stream drives new customers and will be explored for expansion.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=302.0)
      - [**"Outlier" Topics:** High-performing posts (e.g., "G-Wagon tax write-off") will be analyzed for conversion quality to determine if they represent a viable new content avenue.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=400.0)
  - [**Topic Pipeline Management:**](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=158.0)
      - [**Problem:** Topic clusters are imbalanced (e.g., "AI for small business" is robust, "home services" is light), hindering content planning.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=158.0)
      - [**Solution:** Bailey will create an Airtable interface showing all content statuses (in review, in production, published) to provide a complete view for topic selection.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=217.0)

### Brand Voice & Tone Integration

  - [London introduced Relay's new brand guidelines to align content with the "Pathfinder" persona.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=743.0)
  - [**Core Voice Pillars:**](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=976.0)
      - [**Unwavering:** Confident, optimistic.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=989.0)
      - [**Upstanding:** Honest, expert, clear.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=989.0)
      - [**Audacious:** Bold, gritty, confronts business realities.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=989.0)
      - [**Authentic:** Human, conversational, not stiff.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=989.0)
  - [**Tone for Educational Content:** Steady, approachable, and confident, using simple language to avoid jargon.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=1064.0)
  - [**Implementation Plan:**](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=1104.0)
      - [**Goal:** Incrementally improve voice consistency without sacrificing SEO/AEO performance.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=1186.0)
      - [**Method:** GrowthX will update its internal guide using the provided document and "gold standard" examples.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=1775.0)
      - [**Headline Style:** Blog headlines will remain in title case for now to avoid a large, retroactive project.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=1674.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Bailey:**](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=217.0)
      - [Create Airtable interface showing all content statuses.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=217.0)
      - [Deliver January performance report and full progress report next week.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=621.0)
      - [Update GrowthX's internal voice guide and share with London for review.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=1775.0)
  - [**London:**](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=400.0)
      - [Analyze conversion quality for "outlier" topics.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=451.0)
      - [Share brand guide (excluding visuals) and "gold standard" content examples with Dave and Bailey.](https://fathom.video/share/AtWMLgPzbsBEQZ9VD7YWzxVpZ3zghzzB?tab=summary&timestamp=1653.0)
